county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1871 from unorganized territory in northeast Lower Michigan. County seat is Rogers City. Named from French 'presqu'île' (almost an island/peninsula). Limestone quarrying and Lake Huron shipping.
county_metadata.research_tips: Presque Isle County Register of Deeds in Rogers City holds land records from 1871 organization. County Clerk has marriage records and court files. The Presque Isle County Historical Museum has local genealogical materials. Limestone quarrying (Calcite operations, now Carmeuse Lime) was a major industry. German and Polish settlers were numerous. The Great Lakes Lore Maritime Museum in Rogers City documents shipping families. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. BLM GLO land patents. Archives of Michigan has supplementary records. Rogers City newspapers document local families.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1871 to present. Michigan is a public land state -- original federal land patents available through BLM GLO. Register of Deeds holds grantor/grantee indexes, deed books, mortgages, and liens.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estate inventories, guardianship records, and administration files from 1871. Michigan Probate Courts also handled adoption records, mental health commitments, and name changes.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1875 from unorganized territory in north-central Lower Michigan. County seat is Roscommon. Named after County Roscommon, Ireland. Resort county with Houghton Lake and Higgins Lake.
county_metadata.research_tips: Roscommon County Register of Deeds in Roscommon holds land records from 1875 organization. County Clerk has marriage records and court files. The Roscommon County Historical Society has local materials. Lumber industry drove initial settlement. Houghton Lake and Higgins Lake attracted resort development. Irish settlers (the county is named for County Roscommon, Ireland) and German immigrants formed early communities. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. BLM GLO land patents. Archives of Michigan has supplementary records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1835 from Oakland, Macomb, and Shiawassee in east-central Lower Michigan. County seat is Saginaw. Named from Ojibwe for 'place of the Sauk.' One of Michigan's premier lumber capitals.
county_metadata.research_tips: Saginaw County Register of Deeds in Saginaw holds land records from 1835 organization. County Clerk maintains marriage licenses and court records. Probate Court has estate files. The Castle Museum of Saginaw County History has genealogical collections. The Public Libraries of Saginaw have local history and genealogy resources including city directories and newspapers. Saginaw was a lumber capital -- the Saginaw River was one of Michigan's busiest log-driving waterways. German immigrants were the largest ethnic group; check Lutheran and Catholic church records. French-Canadian fur trade families preceded American settlement. Significant African American community from the automobile era. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. Archives of Michigan has Saginaw Valley lumber records.

source_profiles.source_name: Saginaw County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1835 to present. Michigan is a public land state -- original federal land patents available through BLM GLO. Register of Deeds holds grantor/grantee indexes, deed books, mortgages, and liens.

source_profiles.source_name: Saginaw County Probate Records -- Probate Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estate inventories, guardianship records, and administration files from 1835. Michigan Probate Courts also handled adoption records, mental health commitments, and name changes.

source_profiles.source_name: Saginaw County Marriage Records -- County Clerk
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and returns from 1835. Michigan County Clerks issue marriage licenses. State registration of marriages began 1867. Pre-1867 marriages may also be documented in church records.

source_profiles.repository: Walter P. Reuther Library (Wayne State University) / Archives of Michigan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Automobile manufacturing employment records, union (UAW) records, and industrial documentation for Saginaw County. The Walter P. Reuther Library at Wayne State University holds United Auto Workers records and related labor archives. City directories and employee rosters trace worker families. Essential for 20th-century family research in Michigan's industrial counties.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Annual tax rolls listing property owners, assessed values, and personal property. Michigan tax rolls from 1835 at the Archives of Michigan. Essential for tracking land ownership and residency between census years.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historical newspapers for Saginaw County including local weeklies and dailies. Obituaries, legal notices, marriage and death announcements, estate settlements. Michigan Digital Newspaper Archive and Chronicling America have digitized collections.

county_metadata.record_losses: 1881 Great Thumb Fire caused significant record losses
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1848 from St. Clair and Huron in the Thumb region. County seat is Sandusky. Named for a Wyandot chief. 1881 Great Thumb Fire caused significant record losses (burned county).
county_metadata.research_tips: Sanilac County Register of Deeds in Sandusky holds land records, but the 1881 Great Thumb Fire caused significant record losses. This is a burned county. Post-fire records are more complete. The Sanilac County Historical Society and Museum has local genealogical materials and fire-related documentation. German, English, and Canadian settlers formed the primary population. The 1881 fire devastated the entire Thumb region (Sanilac, Huron, Tuscola counties). Check the Archives of Michigan for surviving pre-fire records and BLM GLO for land patents. Church records that survived the fire are essential substitutes. FamilySearch microfilm covers post-fire vital records. Federal census records (1860, 1870) provide pre-fire population data. Check St. Clair and Huron counties (parents).

source_profiles.source_name: Sanilac County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1881 to present. Michigan is a public land state -- original federal land patents available through BLM GLO. Register of Deeds holds grantor/grantee indexes, deed books, mortgages, and liens. Some gaps due to 1881 Great Thumb Fire caused significant record losses.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estate inventories, guardianship records, and administration files from 1881. Michigan Probate Courts also handled adoption records, mental health commitments, and name changes.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and returns from 1881. Michigan County Clerks issue marriage licenses. State registration of marriages began 1867. Pre-1867 marriages may also be documented in church records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1871 from unorganized territory in the central Upper Peninsula. County seat is Manistique. Named for Henry Schoolcraft, explorer and Indian agent. Lumber heritage on the Manistique River.
county_metadata.research_tips: Schoolcraft County Register of Deeds in Manistique holds land records from 1871 organization. County Clerk has marriage records and court files. The Schoolcraft County Historical Society has local materials. Lumber industry along the Manistique River was the primary economic driver. Finnish and French-Canadian settlers formed the main population groups. The Schoolcraft County Pioneer Museum has local history exhibits. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. BLM GLO land patents. Archives of Michigan has UP lumber records. Check Mackinac County for pre-1871 records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1837 from unorganized territory in central Lower Michigan. County seat is Corunna. Named from Ojibwe for 'sparkling water.' Agricultural county with Owosso as a railroad center.
county_metadata.research_tips: Shiawassee County Register of Deeds in Corunna holds land records from 1837 organization. County Clerk maintains marriage licenses from 1837 and court records. Probate Court has estate files. The Shiawassee County Historical Society has genealogical materials. Early settlers came from New York and New England. Owosso was a major railroad center. James Oliver Curwood (author) connections in Owosso. Strong Methodist and Baptist church records. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. BLM GLO land patents. Archives of Michigan has supplementary records. Owosso and Corunna newspapers document local families.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1821 from Macomb County in eastern Lower Michigan. County seat is Port Huron. Named for Lake St. Clair. Border county on the St. Clair River opposite Ontario. Great Lakes shipping heritage.
county_metadata.research_tips: St. Clair County Register of Deeds in Port Huron holds land records from 1821 -- one of Michigan's earliest organized counties. County Clerk maintains marriage records and court files. Probate Court has estate files from the territorial era. The St. Clair County Library has a genealogy department. The Port Huron Museum has local history collections. Port Huron was a major Great Lakes port and border crossing to Sarnia, Ontario. Thomas Edison grew up in Port Huron. Marine City and St. Clair had significant shipbuilding. British Loyalist and Canadian families crossed the border. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. BLM GLO land patents. The Archives of Michigan has territorial records.

source_profiles.source_name: St. Clair County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1821 to present. Michigan is a public land state -- original federal land patents available through BLM GLO. Register of Deeds holds grantor/grantee indexes, deed books, mortgages, and liens.

source_profiles.source_name: St. Clair County Probate Records -- Probate Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estate inventories, guardianship records, and administration files from 1821. Michigan Probate Courts also handled adoption records, mental health commitments, and name changes.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and returns from 1821. Michigan County Clerks issue marriage licenses. State registration of marriages began 1867. Pre-1867 marriages may also be documented in church records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Michigan is a public land state. Original federal land patents for St. Clair County are available free online at BLM GLO. Cash entry, homestead, and military bounty land warrants. Essential for identifying first landowners.

source_profiles.repository: Archdiocese of Detroit Archives / Archives of Michigan / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish records including baptisms, marriages, and burials.  French-Canadian families from the fur trade era are well documented in Catholic registers. FamilySearch has microfilmed many Michigan Catholic parish records.

source_profiles.source_name: St. Clair County Tax Rolls -- Archives of Michigan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Annual tax rolls listing property owners, assessed values, and personal property. Michigan tax rolls from 1821 at the Archives of Michigan. Essential for tracking land ownership and residency between census years.

source_profiles.repository: Archives of Michigan / Library of Congress / Newspapers.com
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historical newspapers for St. Clair County including local weeklies and dailies. Obituaries, legal notices, marriage and death announcements, estate settlements. Michigan Digital Newspaper Archive and Chronicling America have digitized collections.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1829 from unorganized territory in southwest Lower Michigan. County seat is Centreville. Named for the St. Joseph River. Quaker heritage and Underground Railroad activity.
county_metadata.research_tips: St. Joseph County Register of Deeds in Centreville holds land records from 1829 organization. County Clerk maintains marriage records from 1831 and court records. Probate Court has estate files. The St. Joseph County Historical Society has genealogical collections. Early Quaker settlers were active in the Underground Railroad (similar to neighboring Cass County). Potawatomi tribal presence -- the Pokagon Band of Potawatomi has connections here. Strong agricultural community. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. BLM GLO land patents. Archives of Michigan has territorial-period records. Three Rivers and Sturgis newspapers document local families.

source_profiles.source_name: St. Joseph County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1829 to present. Michigan is a public land state -- original federal land patents available through BLM GLO. Register of Deeds holds grantor/grantee indexes, deed books, mortgages, and liens.

source_profiles.source_name: St. Joseph County Probate Records -- Probate Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estate inventories, guardianship records, and administration files from 1829. Michigan Probate Courts also handled adoption records, mental health commitments, and name changes.

source_profiles.source_name: St. Joseph County Marriage Records -- County Clerk
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and returns from 1829. Michigan County Clerks issue marriage licenses. State registration of marriages began 1867. Pre-1867 marriages may also be documented in church records.

source_profiles.source_name: St. Joseph County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for St. Joseph County from 1830 to 1950. Michigan territorial censuses (1827, 1834) may include early residents. Michigan state censuses (1874, 1884, 1894, 1904) provide intercensal data.

source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Michigan is a public land state. Original federal land patents for St. Joseph County are available free online at BLM GLO. Cash entry, homestead, and military bounty land warrants. Essential for identifying first landowners.

source_profiles.source_name: Underground Railroad and African American Records -- St. Joseph County
source_profiles.repository: Archives of Michigan / Burton Historical Collection (Detroit Public Library)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Michigan was a free state, but the Underground Railroad was active throughout southern Michigan with routes to Canada. St. Joseph County records include freedom seekers' documentation, abolitionist society records, African American church records, and community histories. The Burton Historical Collection and Archives of Michigan hold related materials.

source_profiles.source_name: Potawatomi Tribal Records -- St. Joseph County Area
source_profiles.repository: NARA (Great Lakes Agency) / Tribal Government Offices / Archives of Michigan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Treaty records, tribal census rolls (annuity rolls), allotment records, and agency correspondence for Potawatomi communities in St. Joseph County. NARA holds Bureau of Indian Affairs records (Great Lakes Agency). Tribal government offices maintain enrollment records. Archives of Michigan has related territorial records.

source_profiles.source_name: St. Joseph County Tax Rolls -- Archives of Michigan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Annual tax rolls listing property owners, assessed values, and personal property. Michigan tax rolls from 1829 at the Archives of Michigan. Essential for tracking land ownership and residency between census years.

county_metadata.record_losses: 1881 Great Thumb Fire damaged some records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1850 from Sanilac County in the Thumb region. County seat is Caro. Named from a Schoolcraft coinage. 1881 Great Thumb Fire damaged some records (burned county). Sugar beet farming.
county_metadata.research_tips: Tuscola County Register of Deeds in Caro holds land records, though the 1881 Great Thumb Fire damaged some records. This is a burned county, but record losses were less severe than in neighboring Huron and Sanilac counties. County Clerk has marriage records and court files. The Tuscola County Historical Society has local materials. German and Polish immigrants settled heavily in the Thumb area. Sugar beet farming became a major industry. Check the Archives of Michigan for surviving pre-fire records. Church records (Lutheran, Catholic) that survived the fire are essential. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. BLM GLO land patents. Tuscola County Advertiser newspaper archives.

source_profiles.source_name: Tuscola County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1881 to present. Michigan is a public land state -- original federal land patents available through BLM GLO. Register of Deeds holds grantor/grantee indexes, deed books, mortgages, and liens. Some gaps due to 1881 Great Thumb Fire damaged some records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1829 from unorganized territory in southwest Lower Michigan. County seat is Paw Paw. Named for President Martin Van Buren. Fruit farming county. Underground Railroad connections.
county_metadata.research_tips: Van Buren County Register of Deeds in Paw Paw holds land records from 1829 organization. County Clerk maintains marriage records from 1831 and court records. Probate Court has estate files. The Van Buren County Historical Society has genealogical collections. Paw Paw was an early settlement in southwestern Michigan. Underground Railroad routes passed through Van Buren County. The African American community has deep roots (Covert Township). Fruit farming (grapes, berries) attracted diverse settlers. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. BLM GLO land patents. Archives of Michigan has supplementary records.
